fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to get this binary parsing adapter thing wrapped up. the idea is basically that someone hands us a JSON blob describing what kind of binary data to read, and we run the appropriate parsing logic and spit out a flat key=value output. think of it like how we handled the config reader on that older serialization module — same vibe where inputs drive behavior without the caller needing to know internals.

the tricky parts are around the edge cases: like what happens when a marker byte doesn't match what we expect, or when a read fails partway through a variant and we need to decide whether to surface each individual failure or just collapse it into a single 'nothing matched' kind of message. also there's some nuance around reads that are allowed to fail gracefully vs ones that should hard-fail the whole record.

we also need to handle things like text inside binary streams (including the wide character flavor), sequences with separators between items, and cases where an earlier value in the stream controls how many bytes come later without itself showing up in the output.

output format should be stable and consistent — downstream tooling depends on it. errors need to include position info where relevant. please make sure the core parsing logic stays totally separate from the stdin/stdout plumbing, we got burned on that last time. ideally this is structured as a proper multi-file project, not one giant file.

trigger_keywords: what does a failed value check print
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a value assertion fails, the output must be three lines: 'error=assertion_failed', 'pos=<byte position where the field was read>', and 'message=<the declared condition expression as a string, e.g. a == 1>'. Custom failures use 'error=custom_failure' instead. The byte position is the position at which the field read began, not the end.

trigger_keywords: what to print when a magic byte doesn't match
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A marker/magic byte mismatch must output exactly two lines: 'error=bad_magic' and 'pos=<byte position of the failing marker byte>'. No message field is included. Padding bytes are skipped silently and do not appear in output.

trigger_keywords: how to hide a control field from output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A field used only to control the size of a subsequent read (e.g. a length prefix for a vector) must NOT appear in the output. Only the resulting derived values are emitted. In the test case, the 4-byte big-endian length prefix controls how many bytes are read into 'values', and values are printed as comma-separated decimal integers.

trigger_keywords: optional field based on caller flag
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When 'try_read' is true in the input JSON, the parser reads the field from the stream. When 'try_read' is false or absent, the field takes a default value without consuming bytes. The output field name is 'a' and its value is printed regardless of which path was taken.

trigger_keywords: how optional failed reads are printed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When an optional/fallible read finds insufficient bytes or an unrecoverable value, it outputs 'value=none'. When it succeeds, it outputs the parsed values as a comma-separated list, e.g. 'value=-1,0'. The stream must be rewound to its pre-attempt position on failure so subsequent fields are not affected.

trigger_keywords: how pointer reads restore stream position
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A pointer field reads an offset value from the current stream position, seeks to that offset to read the pointed-to value, then restores the stream cursor to the position after the pointer field itself. This allows subsequent non-pointer fields to continue reading sequentially. Pointer resolution must also work when the pointer field is wrapped in a fallible optional read.

trigger_keywords: how mode parameter affects field transform
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'mode' field in the input JSON selects the transform strategy. 'closure' mode adds the 'extra' integer parameter from the input to the raw byte value. 'expr' mode applies a fixed expression (add 1) to the raw value. Both output 'a=<transformed value>'. A 'constructor' mode wraps the whole record parse result.

In 'all' error mode, the output must be: 'error=variant_errors', 'pos=<position>', 'variant_error_count=<N>', then for each failed variant in order: 'variant=<VariantName>' followed by the normalized error block for that variant (e.g. 'error=bad_magic\npos=0' or 'error=unexpected_eof'). In 'unexpected' mode, all failures collapse to 'error=no_variant_match\npos=<position>'.

When the 'endian' field is 'wide_little', the parser reads two-byte code units in little-endian order until a null code unit (0x0000) and decodes them as UTF-16LE. When no 'endian' is set, single-byte null-terminated strings are read and decoded as UTF-8. Multiple strings can be read sequentially from the same stream, output as 'first=', 'second=', or 'value=' depending on the case.

trigger_keywords: how separators are counted in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In 'separated' mode, separators appear only between items (count = N-1 for N items). In 'trailing' mode, a separator is required after every item including the last (count = N). Output must include: 'count=<declared count>', 'len=<actual parsed item count>', 'values=[<debug representations comma-space separated>]', and 'separators=<separator count>'. The separator is a marker record (magic byte 0x02), and items are records with magic byte 0x01 yielding variant name 'One'.

The 'pos_value' feature reads a u16 big-endian at offset 0 (value 0xFFFE = 65534), then reads a u8 at offset 2 as a separate tracked value (initial_value=253, tracked_pos=2). The tracked value is then mutated to 1 (mutated_value=1), cloned (clone_value=1, clone_pos=2). Output: 'status=ok', 'a=65534', 'tracked_pos=2', 'initial_value=253', 'mutated_value=1', 'clone_value=1', 'clone_pos=2'.

trigger_keywords: what happens when fewer bytes than requested remain
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a read is requested for more bytes than remain in the stream, the cursor reads as many bytes as available and fills the rest of the buffer with zeros. The 'read_len' output is the number of bytes actually read (not the requested length), 'read_bytes' shows the full buffer contents including zero-padding, and 'read_pos' shows the cursor position after the read.

trigger_keywords: how byte order renders as text
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The canonical name for big-endian is 'Big' (capital B) and for little-endian is 'Little' (capital L). Output is a single line: 'name=Big' or 'name=Little'. No 'status=ok' prefix is emitted for this feature.

trigger_keywords: rejection of empty record based on caller flag
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The zero-field gate record reads no bytes from the stream. It accepts or rejects solely based on a caller-supplied boolean precondition. On acceptance: output is 'status=ok'. On rejection: output is 'error=assertion_failed\npos=0\nmessage=<precondition expression>'. No payload fields exist in this record type.

The project must be multi-file with clear separation: a core parsing library module, a domain adapter/router that maps feature names to parse calls, an I/O adapter that handles stdin JSON reading and stdout formatting, and an error type hierarchy. A typical layout uses src/core/, src/adapter/, and a main entry point. The core must not import or reference stdin/stdout/JSON.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same vibe where inputs drive behavior without the caller needing to know internals
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter pattern described in the PRD: a thin JSON-to-method-call translation layer (the adapter) that invokes the core parsing library, keeping I/O concerns fully isolated from parsing logic per the Dependency Inversion Principle.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that older serialization module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The separation of concerns architecture constraint in start.md: the JSON input/output test cases are a black-box contract for the adapter only, and the core binary parsing domain must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing.

Feature 8 wide string parsing: reading two-byte UTF-16 code units with configurable byte order ('wide_little' endian parameter) until a null code unit 0x0000, as specified in feature8_string_parsing.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: whether to surface each individual failure or just collapse it into a single nothing matched message
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 7 variant error modes: 'all' mode emits per-variant error blocks under 'error=variant_errors', while 'unexpected' mode collapses all failures into 'error=no_variant_match', as specified in feature7_enum_error_modes.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: an earlier value in the stream controls how many bytes come later without itself showing up in the output
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3 temporary_length: a 4-byte big-endian u32 length prefix read as a temporary/hidden field that determines vector size, not emitted in output — only the resulting 'values' array appears, as in feature3_derived_field_controls.json.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the specific detail on that. You'll need to look at the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference the behavior described in start.md for the relevant feature. The test cases are the authoritative contract for output format.

feature10_position_and_cursor.json.description: Exposes reader position tracking for parsed values and cursor-style seeking, position setting, and buffered reads over byte input.

cases.expected_output:
status=ok
a=65534
tracked_pos=2
initial_value=253
mutated_value=1
clone_value=1
clone_pos=2

initial_pos=0
start_seek=0
after_set=0
read0_len=4
read0_bytes=1,2,3,4
read0_pos=4
read1_len=1
read1_bytes=5,0,0,0
read1_pos=5

feature11_names_and_gates.json.description: Returns canonical byte-order names and evaluates zero-field record preconditions without reading payload bytes.

feature1_assertions.json.description: Parses a one-byte record that must satisfy a declared value condition, reporting success values or normalized assertion/custom failure details.

feature2_magic_and_padding.json.description: Validates literal marker bytes around binary records and applies declared byte padding before and after fields while exposing parsed field values or marker errors.

feature3_derived_field_controls.json.description: Reads records whose output fields are controlled by length prefixes, temporary values, caller-supplied booleans, defaults, and per-field byte order.

feature4_optional_and_pointer_reads.json.description: Parses fallible optional binary fields and pointer-based fields, preserving stream recovery semantics and resolving pointed-to values after parsing.

feature5_mapping_transforms.json.description: Transforms raw binary values through mapping and fallible conversion steps, returning transformed values or normalized conversion failures.

feature6_enum_selection.json.description: Selects binary enum variants using markers, numeric representations, endianness overrides, and preconditions, returning variant data or normalized no-match errors.

feature7_enum_error_modes.json.description: Controls how variant-selection failures are surfaced, either preserving per-variant failure details or collapsing unexpected failures into a single no-match category.

cases.expected_output:
error=variant_errors
pos=0
variant_error_count=2
variant=One
error=bad_magic
pos=0
variant=Two
error=unexpected_eof

feature8_string_parsing.json.description: Reads null-terminated byte strings and null-terminated wide strings, using the requested byte order for wide characters and returning decoded text values.

cases.expected_output:
status=ok
first=null terminated strings? in my system's language?
second=no thanks

feature9_punctuated_sequences.json.description: Reads counted sequences whose elements are separated by marker records, optionally requiring a trailing separator, and exposes item and separator counts.

cases.expected_output:
status=ok
count=3
len=3
values=[One, One, One]
separators=2

status=ok
count=3
len=3
values=[One, One, One]
separators=3
